paper_title: SPIN: Structural LLM Planning via Iterative Navigation for Industrial Tasks

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Industrial LLM agent systems often separate planning from execution, yet LLM planners frequently produce structurally invalid or unnecessarily long workflows, leading to brittle failures and avoidable tool and API cost. We propose \texttt{SPIN}, a planning wrapper that combines validated Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) planning with prefix based execution control. \texttt{SPIN} enforces a strict DAG contract through \texttt{\_validate\_plan\_text} and repair prompting, producing executable plans before downstream execution, and then evaluates DAG prefixes incrementally to stop when the current prefix is sufficient to answer the query. On AssetOpsBench, across 261 scenarios, \texttt{SPIN} reduces executed tasks from 1061 to 623 and improves \emph{Accomplished} from 0.638 to 0.706, while reducing tool calls from 11.81 to 6.82 per run. On MCP Bench, the same wrapper improves planning, grounding, and dependency related scores for both GPT OSS1 and Llama 4 Maverick.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Industrial LLM agent systems often separate planning from execution, yet LLM planners frequently produce structurally invalid or unnecessarily long workflows, leading to brittle failures and avoidable tool and API cost. We propose SPIN, planning wrapper that combines validated Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) planning with prefix based execution control. SPIN enforces strict DAG contract through _validate_plan_text and repair prompting, producing executable plans before downstream execution, and then evaluates DAG prefixes incrementally to stop when the current prefix is sufficient to answer the query. On AssetOpsBench, across 261 scenarios, SPIN reduces executed tasks from 1061 to 623 and improves Accomplished from 0.638 to 0.706, while reducing tool calls from 11.81 to 6.82 per run. On MCP Bench, the same wrapper improves planning, grounding, and dependency related scores for both GPT OSS1 and Llama 4 Maverick.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Industrial asset operations and maintenance workflows are inherently multi-step, cross-functional, and constrained, requiring decisions that are not only correct but also auditable and robust to heterogeneous tool latencies and failures. In benchmarks such as AssetOpsBench, the planners output serves as an interface contract for downstream executors. key practical challenge is that small structural errors in plan, such as incorrect indexing, dependency references, or tool and agent names, can cause hard execution failures and obscure whether the true problem is task reasoning or interface brittleness. Moreover, executing long, conservative workflows end to end can be inefficient when external actions dominate cost and runtime, even though partial progress may already be sufficient to answer the user. These challenges motivate methods that (i) make the plannerexecutor boundary explicit and measurable, and (ii) support cost-aware execution policies that terminate when further actions are unlikely to change the final outcome. Prior work on LLM agents has largely focused on improving action generation, for example through interleaving reasoning with tool use [19, 14] or revising outputs through reflection and self-feedback [13, 9, 18]. However, the plannerexecutor interface is often left implicit, so downstream evaluation can be dominated by brittle formatting errors rather than true task difficulty, especially in domaingrounded benchmarks [12]. Recent evidence further suggests that LLMs cannot be relied upon to consistently verify and repair planning outputs without external checks [15, 6, 2]. Although structured generation can improve schema compliance [5, 10] and search-based methods can use external tests to guide generation [8, 1], these ideas are rarely combined with dependency-consistent task graph representation and cost-sensitive execution policy such as early stopping in domain-grounded agent pipeline. We present SPIN, planning wrapper for tool using LLM agents that makes plan structure an explicit executable interface, instantiated on AssetOpsBench and additionally evaluated on MCP Bench [16]. Preprint. Figure 1: Working example of validation and SPIN system. Starting from planner generated plan_text, SPIN validates and repairs it into dependency aware DAG serialization that an executor can consume deterministically. For execution, SPIN applies simulator and critic based prefix policy, adapted from SPIRAL [20], to stop once the current DAG prefix is sufficient to answer the query. Contributions We develop contract based planning wrapper for tool using LLM agents. Our method combines validator that enforces dependency aware DAG format with prefix evaluation loop in which simulator and critic decide whether further execution is necessary. We present empirical evaluation on two benchmarks with different knowledge settings. The results show improved executability and lower execution burden on AssetOpsBench, and improved planning and grounding related scores on MCP Bench. We provide failure mode analysis showing that the gains of SPIN are concentrated in execution behavior, especially reduced repetition and improved state and termination related control. The analysis also suggests that the simulator is more load bearing component than the critic on the matched scenario set.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: AssetOpsBench is designed for industrial asset operations and maintenance, where operational workflows such as condition monitoring, maintenance planning, and intervention scheduling require coordination across multiple specialized steps, heterogeneous tools, and large operational data sources [12]. This setting differs from many standard agent benchmarks because planner outputs do not remain purely internal reasoning artifacts: they can directly shape downstream execution cost, operational burden, and recovery behavior. In such workflows, the relevant question is not only whether an agent can produce plausible plan, but also whether that plan can be safely consumed and executed within an execution-heavy industrial pipeline. large body of prior work has improved LLM agents through better reasoning, reflection, tool use, or structured generation. Representative examples include interleaving reasoning with acting, verbal reinforcement learning and reflection, dynamic reflection control, decomposed self-correction, reinforcement learning for tool-integrated agents, and tool-interactive critiquing [19, 13, 9, 18, 14, 6]. Related systems work has also emphasized stronger validation and consistency guarantees in multiagent planning pipelines [2]. In parallel, another line of work studies grammar-constrained decoding, schema-constrained outputs, benchmarked structured generation, and schema-level reinforcement learning [4, 5, 10, 11]. These directions are highly relevant to our setting, but our emphasis is different: when agent systems are deployed through external tools in industrial applications, the output must be not only well formed, but also machine-consumable as an execution interface. Accordingly, we treat planner outputs as executable DAG interfaces rather than as generic structured text. complementary line of work evaluates LLM agents on realistic, multi-tool environments via the Model Context Protocol. MCP-Bench probes agent behavior across large suite of MCP servers and heterogeneous real-world tools, measuring tool selection, planning, grounding, and parameter accuracy under task specifications that require multi-hop, cross-domain tool use [16]. Our work shares the view that agents must be evaluated against execution-faithful tool interfaces rather than synthetic APIs, but targets different regime: industrial asset-operations workflows in which planner 2 outputs are consumed downstream as executable DAGs, and where structural validity and execution cost are first-class evaluation criteria. third line of work improves planning through search, tests, simulator-guided reasoning, or more efficient reasoningaction organization [17, 8, 1, 20]. ReWOO reduces redundant interleaving by decoupling reasoning from observations, Thought of Search and AutoToS emphasize efficient and correct search components, and SPIRAL uses plannersimulatorcritic decomposition to improve search over predicted outcomes. Our contribution is closest to this family, but differs in optimization target. We do not primarily aim to improve search quality over imagined futures. Instead, we focus on industrial settings in which downstream execution can dominate practical cost: as language-model inference becomes cheaper and faster over time, the dominant burden may shift toward executable steps, tool invocations, data transfer, API traffic, and recovery-inducing failures, especially in largescale data-intensive workflows. This execution-sensitive perspective is also consistent with recent calls for agent evaluation beyond raw accuracy alone [7]. This is why we treat structural validity and prefix-based control as first-class concerns, and evaluate planner outputs with execution-sensitive criteria in AssetOpsBench.

sections.title: 3 Method
sections.content: We build planning and execution workflow for tool using LLM agents, instantiated on AssetOpsBench and later evaluated on MCP Bench, where planner first produces task graph  (Table 7)  and an evaluation loop determines how much of the plan is necessary to answer the user query (See Figure 1, 4). Our pipeline has two core components (Figure 2): (1) strict plan validator/repair loop that enforces machine readable DAG format, and (2) simulator and critic evaluation loop with early stopping. Our prefix evaluation loop is inspired by the simulator critic decomposition in SPIRAL, but differs in that we operate over validated DAG prefixes and do not use Monte Carlo Tree Search style reflective search. Figure 2: System overview of validated DAG planning and prefix evaluation by Simulator and Critic. 3.1 Formal Problem Setup We study tool-using planning setting in which planner receives user query and produces textual multi-step workflow that must be consumed by downstream executor. We represent parsed plan as an ordered set of nodes = {n1, . . . , nN }, where each node ni = (ti, ai, di, ei) contains task description ti, an assigned agent ai, dependency set di {1, . . . , 1}, and an expected output specification ei. In our implementation, the raw plan text is serialized as four aligned lists: #TaskN, #AgentN, #DependencyN, and #ExpectedOutputN. We treat plan executability as structural validity of this serialized interface prior to downstream execution. Let (x) denote validator that returns validity indicator and an error set, (x) = (valid(x), E(x)). plan is considered valid if it satisfies at least the following constraints: (i) the Algorithm 1 Validation and Repair Loop Require: user query q, planner Π, validator , retry budget Ensure: valid plan or failure after budget exhaustion (valid, E) (x) if valid then return 1: Π(q) 2: for = 0 to do 3: 4: 5: 6: 7: 8: 9: 10: 11: end for end if if = then end if Π(q; E) return FAIL repair conditioned on detected errors four lists have matching lengths, (ii) indices are consecutive from 1 to , (iii) each dependency refers only to valid previous node, and (iv) each assigned agent belongs to predefined admissible set. These constraints define the executable plan interface targeted by our repair loop. Given valid plan p, we evaluate increasingly longer prefixes p(k) = {n1, . . . , nk} rather than executing the entire workflow by default. For each prefix, simulator predicts the candidate outcome of the current execution state, and critic returns structured judgment containing status, Boolean can_answer_now flag, and rationale. This defines stopping policy over plan prefixes: the system halts once the current prefix is judged sufficient to answer the original query q. Our method therefore optimizes two related objectives. The first is executability: increasing the probability that planner outputs are machine-consumable before execution. The second is quality effort trade-off : reducing external execution effort, such as executed tasks, tool calls, API calls, and elapsed time, while preserving sufficient answer quality. Importantly, this objective does not require all forms of cost to decrease simultaneously; in particular, additional simulator and critic prompting may increase internal token usage even when external execution effort decreases. 3.2 Executable DAG Validation and Repair We operationalize plan executability as structural validity of the planner output prior to downstream execution. Rather than directly executing the raw plan_text, we first apply validator that checks alignment across task fields, index consistency, dependency legality, and agent-name admissibility. If the plan is invalid, we feed the detected error set back to the planner and request repaired plan. This produces simple error-aware repair loop whose goal is to transform raw planner outputs into machine-consumable DAG interface. In our implementation, the validator checks whether the four serialized fields (#TaskN, #AgentN, #DependencyN, and #ExpectedOutputN) are aligned, whether indices are consecutive, whether dependencies refer only to valid previous steps, and whether assigned agents belong to an admissible set. The output of this procedure is therefore not task-level success judgment, but pre-execution executability judgment over the plannerexecutor interface. 3.3 Prefix Evaluation with Stopping Given validated plan = {n1, . . . , nN }, we do not execute the full workflow by default. Instead, we evaluate sequence of increasingly longer prefixes p(k) = {n1, . . . , nk}, = 1, . . . , N, and stop as soon as the current prefix is judged sufficient to answer the original user query. This defines execution control as stopping problem over validated DAG prefixes rather than as unconditional end-to-end execution. For each prefix p(k), we decompose the decision into two roles. simulator predicts the candidate outcome of the current execution state without requiring the full downstream execution of later nodes. 4 Algorithm 2 Prefix Evaluation with Stopping Require: validated plan = {n1, . . . , nN }, user query q, simulator S, critic Ensure: final answer ˆy, executed prefix length 1: for = 1 to do 2: construct current prefix p(k) = {n1, . . . , nk} ˆok S(q, p(k)) (statusk, can_answer_nowk, rk) C(q, p(k), ˆok) if can_answer_nowk = {ACCOMPLISHED, PARTIALLYACCOMPLISHED} then 3: 4: 5:

The critic returns structured judgment C(q, p(k), ˆok) = (statusk, can_answer_nowk, rk), where ˆok = S(q, p(k)) is the simulator output, statusk is task-level completion label, can_answer_nowk {True, False} is Boolean sufficiency flag, and rk is short rationale. The stopping decision is therefore given by Stop(k) = 1 (cid:16) statusk {ACCOMPLISHED, PARTIALLYACCOMPLISHED} (cid:17) (cid:16) can_answer_nowk = True (cid:17) . If this condition is not satisfied, the system extends the prefix and repeats the same decision process at the next step. If no earlier prefix satisfies the criterion, the method defaults to the full validated plan. This procedure induces an explicit qualityeffort trade-off. On the one hand, shorter accepted prefixes reduce external execution effort, such as the number of executed tasks, tool calls, API calls, and elapsed runtime. On the other hand, the simulator and critic introduce additional internal prompting effort, measured for example in prompt and completion tokens. Accordingly, SPIN is not designed to reduce every notion of cost simultaneously. Rather, its objective is to reduce costly downstream execution while preserving sufficient answer quality through prefix-level stopping.

sections.title: 4 Experiments
sections.content: We evaluate three points: (i) whether strict DAG validation and targeted repair improve plan executability before downstream execution, (ii) how prefix evaluation changes outcome quality and execution cost on AssetOpsBench, and (iii) how the same planning wrapper behaves on MCP Bench. 4.1 Experimental setup and systems On AssetOpsBench, we evaluate 261 scenarios in total, consisting of 141 original in-distribution (IID) scenarios and 120 additional out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios. The additional OOD scenarios use the same agents and tools as the original benchmark but are instantiated on different assets, so the expanded evaluation should be interpreted not only as larger in-domain test set but also as generalization test under asset shift. Each scenario provides natural language user request and fixed tool and agent environment including IoT metadata, sensor time series, and work order artifacts. Each scenario provides natural language user request and fixed tool and agent environment including IoT metadata, sensor time series, and work order artifacts. We use paired comparisons between the baseline and SPIN based workflow under the same scenario inputs and logging settings. Unless otherwise noted, efficiency metrics are computed over non zero task runs, as reported in Table 2 and Table 3. We compare four systems. [BASE] is the original sequential planning and execution pipeline. [SPIN_wo_sim] removes retrieval conditioned simulation from the prefix loop. [SPIN_wo_cri] weakens critic based stopping. In addition, we evaluate SPIN on 18 two server grounding tasks from MCP Bench using GPT OSS1 (ID 20) and Llama 4 Maverick (ID 16). 4.2 AssetOpsBench results Table 1 reports offline plan format validation results. For each planner configuration, we collect raw plan_text under the baseline prompt (B#*) and the validator assisted setting (V#*), and compute (i) success_rate, the fraction of plans that pass all checks, and (ii) error incidence by rule. The most frequent error types are list-length inconsistencies (COUNTS_MISMATCH ), unsupported agent names ( AGENT_UNKNOWN), and non-consecutive indices across sections (*_NUMBERS_NOT_SEQ). Tables 2 and 3 summarize the end-to-end outcome, scale, and execution-effort metrics on AssetOpsBench. Table 2 shows that, on the same 261 scenarios, [SPIN] reduces the total number of executed tasks from 1061 to 623 while increasing task-level Accomplished from 0.638 to 0.706. At the same time, Not decreases from 0.244 to 0.195, while Error remains low and comparable (0.009 for [BASE] vs. 0.010 for [SPIN]). These results indicate that the reduction in execution is not merely due to premature stopping or degenerate truncation; rather, SPIN executes fewer tasks while preserving and slightly improving task-level outcome quality. Table 3 shows that this quality improvement is accompanied by substantial reduction in downstream execution burden. Relative to [BASE], [SPIN] reduces average tasks per run from 4.07 to 2.39, tool calls per run from 11.81 to 6.82, API calls per run from 34.05 to 19.97, and elapsed runtime from 198.44s to 143.53s. The only major metric that increases is tokens sent per run, from 111,530.3 to 117,592.7, together with tokens received per run from 2590.4 to 5542.3. This pattern is consistent with the design of SPIN: the method spends additional internal prompting budget on simulator and critic calls in order to avoid more expensive downstream execution. We therefore interpret these results not as uniform reduction of all computational cost, but as favorable shift from external execution effort toward internal deliberation. The ablations help clarify which components drive these improvements. Lower is better. Accomplished of 0.665 versus 0.706, and it yields the lowest token-sent value among the SPIN variants at 106,564.0 per run. By contrast, weakening critic-based stopping in [SPIN_wo_cri] leads to longer and more expensive executions: it processes 405 tasks in total, averages 2.87 tasks per run, makes 23.99 API calls per run, and sends 154,002.0 tokens per run. Relative to full [SPIN], these values suggest that the critic contributes directly to limiting execution length once sufficient evidence has been accumulated. At the same time, we interpret the ablations cautiously because they are evaluated on smaller subsets than the full 261-scenario comparison. In particular, while Table 3 suggests that critic weakening most clearly increases execution length, the matched-scenario failure analysis later shows that removing the simulator more strongly degrades robustness-related failure patterns, especially repetition and other specification/state failures. Taken together, these results suggest division of labor between the two components: the critic is more directly responsible for stopping control and executionlength reduction, whereas the simulator contributes more strongly to the quality of intermediate state estimation that supports robust execution behavior. 7 4.3 Failure Mode Analysis on AssetOpsBench Figure 3 reports fine-grained failure incidence rates on the exact common 95-scenario intersection across [BASE], [SPIN], [SPIN_wo_sim], and [SPIN_wo_cri]. This analysis complements the aggregate outcome and effort metrics by showing not only whether SPIN improves execution, but also which types of failures are most affected by each component. Relative to [BASE], full [SPIN] most clearly reduces specification/state-related failures, especially 1.3 Step Repetition, and also lowers 1.5 Unaware of Termination Conditions. This pattern is consistent with the intended role of prefix-based execution control: SPIN suppresses repeated or unnecessarily prolonged execution and improves stopping behavior once the current prefix is already sufficient. In other words, the main robustness gain of SPIN appears in reducing redundant continuation rather than in repairing every downstream failure category equally. At the same time, the figure also shows an important limitation. Failure types such as 2.2 Fail to Ask for Clarification and 3.2 No or Incorrect Verification are not clearly improved by full [SPIN]. One plausible interpretation is that SPIN is explicitly optimized to remove steps that look unnecessary for answering the query under its current internal estimate of sufficiency. As result, steps that would have acted as extra safeguards against missing information or weak verification can also be pruned away as redundant. From this perspective, SPIN becomes more efficient and less repetitive, but does not necessarily become more conservative with respect to clarification or verification failures. Thus, the FMA suggests that SPIN improves execution efficiency and progression control more than it improves robustness to uncertainty-handling or verification-related errors. The ablations further clarify component roles. Removing the simulator causes the failure profile to regress toward [BASE], especially for 1.3 Step Repetition and 3.1 Premature Termination, whereas removing the critic leaves profile that remains comparatively closer to full [SPIN]. This suggests that the simulator is the more critical component for robust intermediate state estimation and stable execution behavior on the matched scenario set, while the critic contributes more directly to execution-length control, as also suggested by the end-to-end effort metrics. 4.4 MCP Bench results Table 4 reports results on 18 two-server grounding tasks from MCP Bench. We evaluate Baseline and SPIN-wrapped runs for GPT-OSS1 (ID 20) and Llama 4 Maverick (ID 16), using the model mapping in Table 6. We include MCP Bench for different reason than AssetOpsBench: in this setting, we do not use any trajectory database or external retrieval memory. As result, MCP Bench tests whether SPIN, and in particular its simulator-centered prefix evaluation, can remain effective when the relevant task knowledge must come primarily from the base LLMs prior knowledge rather than from benchmark-specific stored experience. Under this no-external-memory setting, SPIN still improves most of the quality-oriented metrics for both models. For GPT-OSS1, Task Completion increases from 2.39 to 3.80, Tool Selection from 2.99 to 3.56, Planning Effectiveness from 2.04 to 2.74, Grounding from 2.44 to 3.21, Dependency Awareness from 2.39 to 3.49, and Parallelism & Efficiency from 1.69 to 2.00. For Llama 4 Maverick, Task Completion increases from 2.33 to 2.86, Tool Selection from 2.73 to 3.43, Planning Effectiveness from 1.99 to 2.22, Grounding from 2.54 to 3.19, Dependency Awareness from 2.28 to 2.63, and Parallelism & Efficiency from 1.70 to 1.81. These gains are important because they show that the benefits of SPIN are not limited to settings where the simulator can draw on an external trajectory store. Instead, the same wrapper continues to improve planning and grounding behavior even when its intermediate judgments must be supported primarily by the LLMs internal prior knowledge. The efficiency effects, however, are model-dependent. For GPT-OSS1, Avg Rounds remains unchanged at 7.06, and Prompt Tokens increase slightly from 55,464.78 to 56,890.44. By contrast, for Llama 4 Maverick, SPIN reduces Avg Rounds from 17.06 to 14.00 and Prompt Tokens from 212,118.22 to 125,243.06. We interpret this difference as evidence that SPIN provides useful control mechanism for both models, but that the magnitude of the efficiency benefit depends on the base models native planning and execution behavior. In other words, SPIN improves the quality of plan progression in both cases, while the extent to which this translates into shorter interaction traces or lower token cost is model-specific. 8 Table 4: MCP-Bench results on 18 two-server grounding tasks. We therefore do not claim that SPIN uniformly improves every low-level execution metric. Rather, the overall pattern suggests that SPIN consistently improves higher-level planning and grounding quality, while some lower-level tool-execution behaviors may remain sensitive to the underlying model. Taken together, the MCP-Bench results support stronger claim than simple benchmark transfer: even without any database-backed external memory, SPIN remains effective when the task can be supported by the LLMs prior knowledge alone.

sections.title: 5 Limitations
sections.content: Our evaluation is limited to AssetOpsBench and its fixed tool/agent environment, so the observed quality and efficiency trade-offs needs testing to other benchmarks, or tool APIs with different latency and failure characteristics. In addition, the simulator depends on the coverage, freshness, and representativeness of logged trajectories; sparse or shifting environments can yield inaccurate simulated outcomes that may propagate into suboptimal intermediate decisions. Early stopping further relies on an LLM-based critic, which can be miscalibrated (false positives halting too early; false negatives reducing efficiency) and is sensitive to rubric design, few-shot selection, and model choice, with no formal guarantees on stopping correctness. Finally, SPIN introduces additional components (repair prompting, database retrieval, simulator, critic) that increase engineering complexity and per-step inference overhead.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: We presented SPIN, planning wrapper for tool-using LLM agents that combines executable DAG validation with prefix-based execution control. Across our experiments, SPIN improved planner outputs as machine-consumable interfaces and reduced downstream execution burden while maintaining or improving task quality. On AssetOpsBench, this appeared as both better structural executability and better end-to-end execution efficiency, including fewer executed tasks, tool calls, API calls, and shorter runtimes, while task-level Accomplished also improved. The ablation and failure-mode analyses further suggest that these gains arise primarily from better progression and stopping control: SPIN most clearly reduces redundant continuation and repetition-related failures, while the simulator and critic play different roles in robust state estimation and execution-length control. At the same time, our results also identify an important boundary of the current approach. Clarificationand verification-related failures are not substantially improved, suggesting that SPIN is more effective at pruning unnecessary continuation than at adding robustness-oriented safeguards under uncertainty. natural next step is therefore to extend SPIN toward conditional planning with explicit clarification and fallback branches. More broadly, an interesting future direction is to use the simulator as world model that exposes recurring failure patterns and enables the LLM to learn, through few-shot examples, when task appears risky before execution. This could allow the system not only to stop earlier, but also to recognize in advance when additional clarification, verification, or alternative branches are needed.

sections.title: A Reproducibility Details
sections.content: A.1 Experiment Regeneration Deterministic table recomputation from saved run artifacts. All tables reported in this paper are computed solely from the local artifacts stored under AssetOpsBench/benchmark/- cods_track1/track1_result [3], without re-running the benchmark execution or invoking any external APIs. the table generation scripts read (i) step level execution traces under track1_result/trajectory and (ii) per experiment metadata and summaries under track1_result/exp. Given fixed track1_result directory, this post processing stage is deterministic and therefore reproduces the same aggregate numbers exactly. For example, Table 2 can be regenerated as follows: In particular, AssetOpsBench % python3 make_table_2.py "./benchmark/cods_track1/track1_result/exp" --trajectory_root "./benchmark/cods_track1/track1_result/trajectory" --exp_root --model "Model_16" --tags --out_dir "./benchmark/cods_track1/track1_result/tables3" --debug "BASE,SPIN,SPIN_wo_sim,SPIN_wo_cri" 11 end to end trajectory regeneration via Docker Compose. In addition to the deterministic post processing described above, we provide an end to end replay path that regenerates trajectory (and per-run exp summaries) by re-running the benchmark inside Docker Compose environment. This replay depends on external model/API calls; therefore, we do not guarantee bitwise identical outputs or exact metric equality across runs. Instead, we expect the aggregate statistics (e.g., success rate and error type distributions) to be broadly consistent when the same scenario subset and configuration are used. Configuration. Users must create benchmark/cods_track1/.env.local to provide the required API credentials and endpoints. Secrets must remain local and must not be committed. Run (Compose). # From the repository root (AssetOpsBench/) docker compose -f benchmark/cods_track1/docker-compose.yml up -d --build # (Optional) If you run the benchmark command explicitly in the container: # docker compose -f benchmark/cods_track1/docker-compose.yml exec -T assetopsbench # python /home/run_track_1.py --utterance_ids "<comma-separated IDs>" --save_data "True" The Outputs. unlocal benchmark/cods_track1/track1_result/trajectory der and benchmark/cods_track1/track1_result/exp. All tables in this paper are computed solely from these saved artifacts without re-invoking external APIs. artifacts writes end end run to Failure mode analysis replay via TrajFM. In addition to the benchmark trajectory regeneration above, we provide replay path for the failure mode analysis (FMA) used in the appendix discussion. The FMA pipeline runs the TrajFM extractor separately on saved trajectory directories for [BASE], [SPIN], [SPIN_wo_sim], and [SPIN_wo_cri2], and writes the resulting per-method outputs under track1_result/trajfm_outputs/. This stage depends on external model/API calls and therefore is not guaranteed to be bitwise identical across replays, although the resulting failure distributions are expected to be broadly consistent when the same trajectory inputs and evaluator configuration are used. For consistency, we refer to this variant as [SPIN_wo_cri] in the paper, while the corresponding artifact directory is named spin_wo_cri2/work/ in the released outputs. Run (TrajFM). The extraction step is executed inside the benchmark container with: # From the repository root (AssetOpsBench/) docker compose -f benchmark/cods_track1/docker-compose.yml exec -T assetopsbench bash /home/entrypoint_failure_modes_analysis.sh This entrypoint activates from /home/track1_result/trajectory/, and runs TrajFM/failure_mode_extractor.py separately for each method directory. the benchmark environment, saved trajectories reads Outputs. The FMA extraction writes method specific outputs under: benchmark/cods_track1/track1_result/trajfm_outputs/ base/work/ spin/work/ spin_wo_sim/work/ spin_wo_cri2/work/ These directories contain the raw TrajFM outputs used for the matched scenario analysis in Appendix B. Figure generation. After the FMA counts and rates are computed, make_fma_figure.py generates the rate figures used in the paper: # From the repository root (AssetOpsBench/) python make_fma_figure.py the plotting script This script writes: benchmark/cods_track1/track1_result/trajfm_outputs/fma_full_rate.pdf benchmark/cods_track1/track1_result/trajfm_outputs/fma_category_rate.pdf The current plotting script renders figures from precomputed aggregated rates; it does not itself perform the raw trajectory labeling or aggregation. External API dependency note. Our model routing / numeric model identifiers for external calls follow the implementation in ReActXens model_inference.py1. If the provider changes the identifier mapping or discontinues service, exact replay may become impossible, and only partial reproducibility from saved artifacts is guaranteed. sections.title: Compose
sections.content: A.3 Fixed Experimental Settings (Configuration Only) Evaluation scenario sets (fixed). We use different fixed evaluation sets for different parts of the paper. For the offline DAG validation study in Table 1, we evaluate 141 plan files per planner configuration. For the end to end AssetOpsBench results in Table 2 and Table 3, we evaluate 261 scenarios. For the MCP Bench results in Table 4, we evaluate 18 two server grounding tasks. Within each table, all reported aggregates are computed over the corresponding fixed scenario set. Outcome sensitive knobs (fixed). The reported aggregates depend on the following configuration choices: SPIN iteration / validation budget (T ). The number of SPIN iterations is controlled in AssetOpsBench/src/agent_hive/workflows/ track1_planning_{...}.py (denoted as T= in the code). Changing changes both cost and outcomes. Simulator retrieval filter. The Simulator retrieves task summaries restricted to Accomplished entries, that is, only completed historical tasks are used as retrieval candidates. Changing this filter, for example by including Not accomplished cases, may change Simulator outputs. Early stopping rule (Critic stop). We enable early stopping when the Critic returns can_answer_now=True for tasks that the Simulator predicts as Accomplished or Partially_Accomplished. Restricting the condition to only Accomplished is an alternative design choice and can change outcomes. Data persistence and feedback effects (save_data). When save_data is enabled, generated plans and trajectories are written to the database and become queryable by downstream components. This can affect later Simulator retrieval behavior, and thus end to end results, by changing the database state seen by retrieval. External API conditions (model identification and logging). When reproducing results that require external LLM APIs, we identify the exact provider and model via our internal LLM_ID. Table 6 maps each LLM_ID to the corresponding model string used in the implementation, together with provider and deployment details. For each external API call, we log (i) timestamp, (ii) LLM_ID and provider/model identifier, (iii) inference parameters such as temperature, top-p, and max_tokens, and (iv) retry and timeout metadata such as retry count and timeout type. These records enable post hoc verification of what could vary across runs, including provider model updates, stochastic decoding, and rate limit induced retries or timeouts. A.4 Failure mode analysis on matched scenarios Scope and comparison setting. We conduct failure mode analysis on AssetOpsBench trajectories from [BASE], [SPIN], [SPIN_wo_sim], and [SPIN_wo_cri]. To ensure fair comparison, we restrict the analysis to the exact common utterance ID intersection across all four methods. After collapsing to one trajectory per utterance ID, this yields 95 shared scenarios. ID 1.1 1.2 1.3 1.4 1. 2.1 2.2 2.3 2.4 2.5 2. 3.1 3.2 3.3 Table 5: Failure taxonomy used in the matched scenario failure mode analysis. Category Definition Specification and state Specification and state Specification and state Specification and state Specification and state Interaction Interaction Interaction Interaction Interaction Interaction Disobey Task Specification: the trajectory does not follow the requested task or violates explicit task constraints. Disobey Role Specification: the agent acts outside its assigned role or uses behavior inconsistent with the role definition. Step Repetition: the trajectory repeats the same or nearly identical step without making meaningful progress. Loss of Conversation History: the agent ignores or forgets relevant context established earlier in the interaction. Unaware of Termination Conditions: the agent fails to recognize when the task should stop or when enough information has already been obtained. Conversation Reset: the trajectory restarts the interaction or behaves as if prior turns were unavailable. Fail to Ask for Clarification: the agent proceeds without requesting clarification even though the available information is insufficient or ambiguous. Task Derailment: the interaction drifts away from the intended objective and focuses on irrelevant or secondary subproblems. Information Withholding: relevant information that was obtained is not surfaced in the response or not used when it should be. Ignored Other Agents Input: outputs or observations from previous agents or earlier steps are not incorporated when they are relevant to the next action. ActionReasoning Mismatch: the stated reasoning and the executed action are inconsistent with each other. Verification and completion Verification and completion Verification and completion Premature Termination: the trajectory ends before enough evidence has been collected to support the answer. No or Incorrect Verification: the agent does not verify an important claim, or performs verification step incorrectly. Weak Verification: verification is attempted, but it is incomplete, shallow, or insufficiently connected to the final conclusion. Model and evaluation prompt. Failure labels were assigned by an LLM based evaluator using internal LLM_ID 12  (Table 6)  . For each trajectory, the evaluator receives the user question, the executed trajectory, and the final answer, and returns structured multi label judgement over the predefined failure taxonomy described below. Failure taxonomy. We use three category failure taxonomy covering specification and state related failures (1.x), interaction failures (2.x), and verification and completion failures (3.x). Table 5 defines all failure modes used in the analysis.

sections.title: B Failure Mode Analysis Details
sections.content: B.1 Failure mode analysis on matched scenarios Scope and comparison setting. We conduct failure mode analysis on AssetOpsBench trajectories from [BASE], [SPIN], [SPIN_wo_sim], and [SPIN_wo_cri]. To ensure fair comparison, we restrict the analysis to the exact common utterance ID intersection across all four methods. After collapsing to one trajectory per utterance ID, this yields 95 shared scenarios. Model and evaluation prompt. Failure labels are assigned by an LLM based evaluator using fixed prompt and predefined failure taxonomy. We use the same evaluation setup across all four methods so that differences in reported failure rates reflect differences in the trajectories rather than differences in the labeling procedure. We used model 12 as the evaluator  (Table 6)  . 15 Table 6: Mapping from our internal LLM IDs to the model strings used in ReActXens model_inference.py, together with provider and deployment notes. Unless explicitly stated, quantization and low level serving optimizations are provider managed and not disclosed. Decoding parameters follow our implementation defaults when no overrides are provided: temperature = 0.0 and max tokens = 8192 for text generation. ID Model string in ReActXen Provider Size (total / active) Decoding (temp / max tok) 0.0 / 8192 70B (dense) 400B total / 17B active 0.0 / 8192 12 16 20 22 38 meta-llama/llama-3-3-70b-instruct meta-llama/ llama-4-maverick-17b-128e-instruct-fp8 openai/gpt-oss-120b Meta Meta OpenAI 117B total / 5.1B active 0.0 / 8192 mistralai/mistral-medium-2505 Mistral AI (see provider docs) 0.0 / mistralai/ mistral-small-3-1-24b-instruct-2503 ibm/granite-4-h-small Mistral AI 24B (dense) 0.0 / 8192 IBM 32B total / 9B active 0.0 / 8192 Quantization / Hosting Provider default; self hostable open weight Provider default; self hostable (license gated) Provider default; self hostable open weight Provider default; hosted API Provider default; open weight + hosted options Provider default; self hostable open weight Aggregation and reporting protocol. For each utterance ID, we aggregate failure labels across repeated annotations by OR aggregation, so that failure mode is counted as present if it appears at least once for that scenario. We report both fine grained failure rates over the full taxonomy and category level aggregates over 1.x, 2.x, and 3.x groups. We also report the mean number of failure modes per scenario on the same matched scenario set. Results on the matched scenario set. Figure 3 report failure incidence rates on the exact common 95 scenario intersection across [BASE], [SPIN], [SPIN_wo_sim], and [SPIN_wo_cri]. Under this strict matched comparison, full [SPIN] has the lowest mean number of failure modes per scenario at 1.48, compared with 1.87 for [BASE], 1.80 for [SPIN_wo_sim], and 1.53 for [SPIN_wo_cri]. The clearest differences appear in 1.x specification and state related failures. In category totals, 1.x decreases from 60 for [BASE] to 27 for full [SPIN], while [SPIN_wo_sim] and [SPIN_wo_cri] have 41 and 33, respectively. Within this category, the largest single reduction is 1.3 Step Repetition, which drops from 35.79% in [BASE] to 10.53% in full [SPIN]; the corresponding rates are 17.89% for [SPIN_wo_sim] and 12.63% for [SPIN_wo_cri]. 1.5 Unaware of Termination Conditions also decreases from 23.16% in [BASE] to 15.79% in full [SPIN]. For 3.x verification and completion failures, the category totals are 84 for [BASE], 78 for full [SPIN], 93 for [SPIN_wo_sim], and 77 for [SPIN_wo_cri]. At the fine grained level, 3.2 No or Incorrect Verification remains high for all methods, but is lower for full [SPIN] than for [BASE] (47.37% vs. 50.53%). 3.3 Weak Verification is also lower for full [SPIN] than for [BASE] (9.47% vs. 12.63%). In contrast, 3.1 Premature Termination is identical for [BASE], full [SPIN], and [SPIN_wo_cri] at 25.26%, while [SPIN_wo_sim] is higher at 37.89%. The 2.x interaction failures vary less across methods. The category totals are 34 for [BASE], 36 for full [SPIN], 37 for [SPIN_wo_sim], and 35 for [SPIN_wo_cri]. In particular, 2.2 Fail to Ask for Clarification is 24.21% for [BASE], 26.32% for full [SPIN], 24.21% for [SPIN_wo_sim], and 26.32% for [SPIN_wo_cri].

sections.title: C Working Examples
sections.content: C.1 Various DAG examples To provide concrete, pictorial intuition for our DAG plans, we include worked example and compact catalog of plan shapes in Appendix C. Figure 4 visualizes four step maintenance decision plan, showing how step indices (S1S4) in the plan text map to the dependency graph and the agent assigned to each step. Table 7 complements this by presenting multiple representative dependency patterns (e.g., simple path, tree, and general DAG structures), illustrating that the same plan format can express both sequential workflows and more complex multi branch dependencies. 16 C.2 _validate_plan_text Table 8 summarizes synthetic unit tests for our plan-text validator. The validator enforces strict, execution ready schema (#TaskN, #AgentN, #DependencyN, #ExpectedOutputN) before any downstream tool calls are issued. We construct minimally perturbed plans to exercise each validation rule, and we report the resulting is_valid flag together with representative error messages. These checks make common failure modes explicit (e.g., missing fields, non-consecutive indices, malformed dependencies, forward references, or unknown agent names) and prevent malformed plans from entering the execution pipeline. C.3 Simulator Agent Table 9 provides concrete example of the SimulatorAgent, which serves as retrieval surrogate world model for predicting the expected output of planned step. Given the user question and the target agent/task description, the SimulatorAgent retrieves similar past trajectories and prompts an LLM to produce predicted output for the current step. We compare the predicted answer against known ground-truth answer for this test case, and we additionally report input/output token counts to quantify the compute cost of single simulation call. This example illustrates how simulation can yield near correct intermediate results while also exposing the overhead (tokens) introduced by retrieval and prompting. C.4 Critic Agent Table 10 reports CriticAgent outcomes for two provided examples (as observed in our docker logs). The CriticAgent evaluates prefix of the planned DAG and candidate answer, and returns structured JSON judgement: status (Accomplished, Partially accomplished, Not accomplished), can_answer_now, and short rationale. Example demonstrates Not accomplished decision when the truncated plan targets the wrong site, while Example shows Partially accomplished when the candidate answer contains the correct asset list but is mixed with irrelevant information. We also report token usage (input/output) because the Critic may be invoked iteratively during prefix evaluation; in our implementation, we treat Accomplished or Partially accomplished as success flag for early stopping. C.5 Execution Agents Design intent. In AssetOpsBench, each domain execution agent is shipped with role specific system prompt that enumerates the tools it can invoke (e.g., sites, assets, sensors, history) and the expected I/O schema. During execution, the agent follows ReAct style loop (Thought ActionObservation) and often returns file backed tool outputs (JSON artifacts) to avoid emitting large payloads directly in the chat response. Reflexion and review. Optionally, an additional review step (Reflexion) provides self reflection and task level judgement (e.g., Accomplished) after the agent finishes. Highlighted excerpt (simplified). The following excerpt is simplified execution trace for the IoT Data Download agent. It demonstrates (i) tool selection, (ii) parameterized tool invocation, and (iii) file-backed outputs referenced in the final answer. ************ ReactReflectAgent ************ Question: what assets are at site MAIN Thought 1: need to get the list of assets to answer the question. Action 1: assets Action Input 1: site_name=MAIN Observation 1: "site_name":"MAIN","total_assets":6, "file_path":"/tmp/cbmdir/990f...0.json", "message":"found 6 assets for site_name MAIN ..." Thought 2: now have the list of assets stored in JSON file. Action 2: Finish Action Input 2: The list of assets at the MAIN site is stored in /tmp/cbmdir/990f15f7-45a5-4eac-ac48-6a3cfb600af0.json. Agent is Enabled with Reflexion Review Agent: status: Accomplished, reasoning: ...

sections.title: D Discussion of Our Work
sections.content: D.1 Research Hypotheses RQ1 (Overthinking): Do LLM planners overthink in industrial workflow planning, and can we mitigate it without sacrificing task accomplishment? RQ2 (Early stopping): Does integrating prefix evaluation early stopping by World-Model and Critic reduce effort while maintaining or improving success? D.2 What the Results Show Main empirical findings. The main empirical result is that [SPIN] improves quality and reduces external execution effort on AssetOpsBench, while also transferring to MCP Bench under different simulator setting. On AssetOpsBench (Table 2 and Table 3), [SPIN] increases Acc from 0.638 to 0.706 and decreases Not from 0.244 to 0.195, while reducing total executed tasks from 1061 to 623 and average tasks per run from 4.07 to 2.39. At the run level, tool calls decrease from 11.81 to 6.82, API calls from 34.05 to 19.97, and elapsed time from 198.44s to 143.53s. Tokens sent per run increase from 111,530.3 to 117,592.7, indicating that the simulator and critic introduce additional internal prompting overhead even as they reduce downstream execution effort. As described in Section E.2, the Simulator is designed as retrieval conditioned world model that predicts step outcomes from prior task experience. This interpretation is supported by the ablations and by the matched scenario failure analysis in Appendix B: removing the simulator causes clearer regression in failure structure, especially in repetition and termination related failures. At the same time, the additional 120 AssetOpsBench scenarios use the same agents and tools as the original 141 scenarios but are defined over different assets, and the retrieval database does not contain trajectory data for those additional assets. The fact that [SPIN] still improves results on the expanded 261 scenario set therefore suggests that the simulator is not acting only as asset specific memory, but is also transferring task level knowledge across related settings. This interpretation is further supported by the MCP Bench results in Table 4. In MCP Bench, we do not use retrieval database, because the benchmark is designed around general knowledge and multi tool coordination rather than benchmark specific trajectory memory. Even under this setting, SPIN improves Task Completion, Tool Selection, Planning Effectiveness, Grounding, Dependency Awareness, and Parallelism & Efficiency for both GPT-OSS1 and Llama 4 Maverick. For GPTOSS1, Avg Rounds remains unchanged at 7.06 and prompt tokens increase slightly, whereas for Llama 4 Maverick, Avg Rounds decreases from 17.06 to 14.00 and prompt tokens decrease from 212,118.22 to 125,243.06. These results indicate that SPIN can improve planning outcomes not only when retrieval based world model is available, but also when the benchmark domain is covered mainly by the LLMs general knowledge. Taken together, the evidence suggests that SPIN combines two useful effects: retrieval based task knowledge transfer when trajectory memory is available, and improved planning control even without benchmark specific memory. Matched scenario failure analysis. The matched scenario failure analysis in Appendix provides more detailed view of where the gains arise. On the exact common 95 scenario intersection across [BASE], [SPIN], [SPIN_wo_sim], and [SPIN_wo_cri], full [SPIN] achieves the lowest mean failure count per scenario at 1.48, compared with 1.87 for [BASE], 1.80 for [SPIN_wo_sim], and 1.53 for [SPIN_wo_cri]. The clearest reduction appears in specification and state related failures, especially 1.3 Step Repetition, which drops from 35.79% in [BASE] to 10.53% in full [SPIN]. The corresponding rate rises to 17.89% in [SPIN_wo_sim] and 12.63% in [SPIN_wo_cri]. These results show that the main empirical effect of [SPIN] is not only shorter execution, but also more stable and less repetitive task progression. 18 Component level interpretation. The ablations suggest that the simulator is the more load bearing component for robustness on the matched scenario set. Removing the simulator weakens much of the advantage of full [SPIN] and moves the system closer to [BASE] in average failure count, repetition related failures, premature termination, and verification and completion totals. By contrast, [SPIN_wo_cri] remains comparatively close to full [SPIN]. At the same time, the matched scenario analysis also shows limitation: [SPIN] does not improve clarification related behavior. In particular, 2.2 Fail to Ask for Clarification is not reduced relative to [BASE]. This suggests that the gains of [SPIN] come mainly from stronger control of execution flow and reduced redundant behavior, rather than from more cautious handling of missing information. Relation to prior work. Prior work on LLM agents has mainly emphasized reasoning quality, search quality, or token efficiency, for example by interleaving reasoning with tool use, adding reflection or self feedback, enforcing structured outputs, or using external tests and simulator based search to improve planning quality [19, 14, 13, 9, 18, 5, 10, 8, 1]. Our results suggest different framing for tool using industrial settings. When downstream execution dominates cost, structural validity and prefix control become first class concerns. In this setting, the main burden is not only how many reasoning tokens the model uses, but also how many executable steps, tool calls, API calls, and recovery inducing failures are produced downstream. This is why we treat the planner output as an executable DAG interface and evaluate it with execution sensitive criteria in AssetOpsBench [12]. Relative to SPIRAL style planner, simulator, critic decomposition, our contribution is not to improve search over predicted outcomes per se, but to enforce machine consumable plan structure and reduce downstream execution burden through prefix based control [20]. D. Impact of Our Work Execution centric planning. Under this framing, our main claim is that in tool using industrial environments, external execution burden is often more important than reasoning length or token efficiency alone. When downstream execution dominates cost, structural validity and prefix control become first class concerns. Our results suggest that planner outputs should be treated not only as reasoning artifacts, but as executable interfaces whose quality must be evaluated through executed tasks, tool and API calls, elapsed time, and downstream failure patterns. What SPIN changes in this setting. From this perspective, the contribution of SPIN is not merely that it generates shorter plans, but that it improves executability and execution behavior under execution sensitive evaluation. The validator makes planner outputs machine consumable before execution, while the prefix control mechanism reduces avoidable downstream work. The simulator can also be interpreted as world model like component: on AssetOpsBench, improvements remain even when the additional 120 scenarios involve assets not covered by the retrieval database, which suggests task level knowledge transfer beyond asset specific memory; on MCP Bench, improvements are also observed without any retrieval database, which suggests that the same framework remains useful when the benchmark domain is covered mainly by the LLMs general knowledge. Implications and next steps. These findings suggest that practical progress in industrial agent systems may come less from making plans longer or more elaborate, and more from making them structurally valid, execution aware, and just sufficient for the task. At the same time, our results also show that this efficiency oriented control does not automatically improve clarification or fallback behavior. natural next step is therefore conditional planning with explicit fallbacks, where DAG plans are extended with contingency branches that preserve robustness while keeping execution cost controlled.

sections.title: E System details
sections.content: E.1 _validate_plan_text _validate_plan_text is pre-execution gate that checks whether an LLMRole in the pipeline. generated plan_text satisfies the required marker-based structure and basic consistency constraints. Only validated plans are passed to downstream execution (and any prefix evaluation). For invalid plans, the validator returns human-readable error list that can be used to trigger repair or regeneration. 19 Inputs are plan_text: str and agents_allowed (a collection of Input / Output contract. permitted agent names). The function returns tuple (is_valid: bool, errors: List[str]), where is_valid is True iff no violations are found, and errors accumulates all detected issues as strings. Expected plan format and parsing. The validator assumes four-line tagged format per step i: #Taski:, #Agenti:, #Dependencyi:, and #ExpectedOutputi:. It extracts (index, content) pairs for each tag type using anchored regular expressions in multiline mode, and then performs consistency checks over the extracted sequences. Constraints enforced. The implementation enforces: (i) presence of each tag type, (ii) per-tag index sequences must be exactly 1..N in increasing order, (iii) equal counts across Task/Agent/Dependency/ExpectedOutput blocks, (iv) dependency syntax must be either None or whitespace-separated list of tokens #Sk, (v) dependency indices must lie in 1..N and must not include forward/self references (k i), and (vi) agent names must be in agents_allowed. Error reporting. Violations are appended to errors (the validator attempts to continue checking to report multiple issues at once). Representative failure cases and the corresponding key error messages are summarized in Table 8. E.2 Simulator Agent Role in the pipeline. The Simulator is retrieval-conditioned surrogate that predicts the textual output an execution agent would produce for single target task. It is invoked during prefix-based evaluation to estimate whether partial plan already yields enough information to answer the user. The Simulator itself does not implement early stopping decisions or uncertainty handling; it only produces predicted output string for the specified task. Input / Output contract. The public entry point is run(user_question, task_description, agent_name, dag_prefix=None). Inputs are: (i) the original user question, (ii) target task description, (iii) the execution agent name for that task, and (iv) an optional DAG prefix (previous steps) which is stringified and provided as context. The function returns the predicted output text; the implementation additionally returns token accounting from the LLM wrapper (input and generated token counts) as auxiliary metadata. Retrieval backend and data schema. The Simulator retrieves similar historical tasks from the PostgreSQL table traj_task_summaries. Each retrieved row is represented as fixed-schema dictionary (TaskSummaryHit via TypedDict) containing identifiers, task metadata, natural-language summary, and similarity score/rank. Similarity search (pgvector). Given query embedding q, the Simulator performs nearestneighbor search over summary_vec using pgvector distance operators and orders results by increasing distance, returning the top-k hits. The SQL applies optional filters for agent_name and status (e.g., restricting to Accomplished tasks) before the ORDER BY ... LIMIT stage. Query construction and embedding. The similarity query text is constructed compactly as: User question, Agent, and Task fields concatenated with newlines. This text is embedded by an external embedder function (loaded from summarize.py) to match the dimensionality of summary_vec. The resulting float vector is serialized into pgvector literal (e.g., [1,2,3]) and passed to SQL as typed parameter (cast to ::vector). Prompt construction (context packing). The Simulator prompt is assembled in the following order: (1) Simulator system_prompt, (2) the User Question, (3) an optional DAG Prefix section listing prior steps, (4) set of ground-truth trajectories provided verbatim as few-shot exemplars, (5) the Target Task (agent + description), (6) compact list of retrieved Similar Past Tasks (status + summary), and (7) final instruction requiring the model to output only the predicted result (no explanations, no JSON). The exact full prompt text (including section headers and the final instruction) is provided in Appendix E.4 (Figure 5). 20 Database construction. For AssetOpsBench, the Simulator retrieval database was constructed from saved trajectories generated on the following scenario IDs: {3, 4, 5, 41, 42, 43, 109, 110, 111, 112, 113, 114, 120, 219, 220, 221, 222, 223, 400, 401, 402, 414, 415, 416, 426, 427, 428, 434, 435, 511, 512, 513, 514, 515, 612, 613, 614, 615, 622}. The resulting trajectories were stored in PostgreSQL and used as retrieval candidates for the Simulator. The original 141 scenarios and the additional 120 scenarios use the same agents and tools, but they are defined over different assets. Our retrieval database contains trajectory data only for the assets appearing in the original 141 scenarios, and does not include trajectory data for the assets introduced in the additional 120 scenarios. The latter scenarios can be inspected from the public dataset release at https://huggingface.co/datasets/ ibm-research/AssetOpsBench/viewer/hydrolic_pump. By contrast, we did not use retrieval database for MCP Bench, because MCP Bench is designed around general knowledge and multi tool coordination tasks, so we evaluated the system without trajectory based retrieval support in the Simulator. Database access and resource handling. The Simulator connects to PostgreSQL using psycopg3 and manages both connections and cursors via context managers, ensuring proper close semantics and transactional commit/rollback behavior on scope exit. Generation parameters. The LLM call is made through wrapper (watsonx_llm) with fixed model selector (e.g., model_id=16 is the default in the current implementation). Unless explicitly overridden in the wrapper, decoding parameters are treated as defaults; the Simulator enforces strict output policy via the prompt (Respond ONLY with the predicted output). Failure handling and graceful degradation. If no similar tasks are retrieved (empty hit list), the Similar Past Tasks section is omitted and the model is prompted using only the system prompt, user question, target task, optional prefix, and few-shot exemplars. If the database URL is missing, initialization fails fast with configuration error (requiring DB_URL / DATABASE_URL). Full prompt. For reproducibility, we disclose the Simulator prompt verbatim as used in our implementation (system prompt + context packing template) in Appendix E.4, Figure 5. E.3 Critic Agent Role in the pipeline. The Critic evaluates whether candidate answer (produced by another component) is sufficient to answer the user question given the current DAG prefix (steps planned or executed so far). It supports early stopping by deciding whether the system can answer at the current prefix. public contract. The dag_prefix, evaluate(user_question, Input / Output candidate_answer, the user quesInputs are: tion, candidate answer string, DAG prefix (a sequence of strings and/or dictionaries), and optional scenario metadata. The Critic returns fixed-schema JSON object with exactly three keys: status (Accomplished / Partially accomplished / Not accomplished), can_answer_now (boolean), and rationale (short explanation). The implementation additionally returns token accounting (input/generated tokens). is entry scenario_context=None). point Few-shot rubric construction from ground-truth trajectories. Few-shot examples are built offline from GROUND_TRUTH_TRAJECTORIES_JSON. For each trajectory, we extract text as the user question and build compact DAG prefix as list of strings of the form "Agent -> action(args)" from execution_steps. If the trajectory contains Finish step, the examples candidate answer is set to Finish.argument; trajectories without Finish (or missing question/answer strings) are skipped. Each retained example is labeled as status=Accomplished and can_answer_now=True. (context construction Prompt (1) CRITIC_SYSTEM_PROMPT (role + strict JSON schema), (2) Few-shot evaluation examples (each example includes question, DAG prefix, candidate answer, and the expected evaluation JSON), (3) New case to evaluate (current question, DAG prefix, candidate answer), (4) optional scenario_context serialized as JSON, and (5) final instruction to output only single JSON packing). The Critic concatenates: prompt 21 object with keys status, can_answer_now, and rationale. The full prompt template is provided in Appendix E.4, Figure 6. DAG prefix representation. Prefix elements may be strings or dictionaries. Strings are emitted verbatim. Dictionaries are formatted into single line using (when present) the fields agent, task, dependency, and expected_output. In our pipeline, helper parser converts plan text in the #Task/#Agent/#Dependency/#ExpectedOutput format into this dictionary form. Generation, parsing, and safeguards. The Critic calls the LLM via watsonx_llm. The prompt enforces structured output (valid JSON only, no extra fields, no markdown). The implementation parses the model output as JSON; on failure, it attempts to extract the substring between the first { and last } and parse again. The result is sanitized to ensure valid status, boolean can_answer_now, and non-empty rationale; if parsing fails, the Critic returns safe default (Not accomplished, can_answer_now=False). Early-stopping logic and ablation hook. The Planner consumes status and can_answer_now to decide early stopping at the current prefix (the Critic does not output stop_index in this implementation). To disable the Critic, we bypass evaluation (or force can_answer_now=False), yielding no early stop baseline with longer executions and higher token/call usage. E.4 Prompts by Agent Role Simulator agent prompt. Figure 5 presents the complete Simulator prompt (system instruction + assembled context) used in our pipeline. The prompt specifies the Simulators role as retrieval conditioned surrogate: given the user question, an optional DAG prefix, target task (agent name and description), and optionally (i) few-shot ground-truth trajectories and (ii) retrieved summaries of similar past tasks, it must predict the target agents final textual output and return only that output (no reasoning or structured formats). We include the prompt verbatim to support reproducibility, as small prompt-format changes can materially affect model behavior. Critic agent prompt. Figure 6 shows the complete Critic prompt (system instruction + assembled context) used for prefix-based evaluation and early stopping decisions. The prompt (i) defines the Critics role as judging candidate answer against the user question conditioned on the current DAG prefix, (ii) constrains the output to single JSON object with keys status (Accomplished / Partially accomplished / Not accomplished), can_answer_now (boolean), and short rationale, and (iii) provides few-shot evaluation exemplars, followed by the new case (optionally including scenario context for reference). We include the prompt verbatim to support reproducibility because prompt formats and small instruction changes can materially affect LLM behavior and evaluation outcomes. E.5 _build_repair_prompt Planner repair prompt. Figure 7 presents the complete repair prompt constructed by _build_repair_prompt and provided to the planner LLM when we request minimally revised DAG plan. The function assembles four components: (i) the original planning prompt with the output marker removed to avoid duplication, (ii) an optional SPIN-style evaluation summary (status, rationale, and optionally can_answer_now and stop_index), (iii) validator-detected issues from _validate_plan_text, and (iv) the original plan text. When available, we additionally include truncated plan prefix derived from stop_index and instruct the planner to treat it as the current repair context. The appended repair rules explicitly bias the planner toward the bare minimum plan: it must incorporate SPIN feedback, resolve structural issues, and make only minimal changesoutputting the original plan verbatim if no fixes are required. We report the full prompt verbatim to support reproducibility, as even small changes in feedback formatting or repair constraints can affect planning behavior. Figure 7: Planner repair prompt (base prompt + SPIN feedback + validator issues + original plan). 22 <BASE_PROMPT_WITH_OUTPUT_MARKER_REMOVED> [NOTE: This is the original base prompt with the line "Output (your generated plan) :" removed exactly once.] === SPIN Evaluation Feedback === [CASE A: spiral_feedback is provided] SPIN-style evaluation of the current plan: - Status: <STATUS> - can_answer_now: <CAN_ANSWER_NOW> - stop_index: <STOP_INDEX> [default: "Unknown"] [OPTIONAL: included only if present] [OPTIONAL: included only if present] (earliest step index after which SPIN believes the plan can already answer) - Critic rationale: <RATIONALE> [OPTIONAL: if truncated_plan_text is provided, include the following] Truncated plan (use this as the current plan context for repair): <TRUNCATED_PLAN_TEXT> [CASE B: spiral_feedback is not provided] SPIN-style evaluation of the current plan is not available for this round. Assume the current plan may still be suboptimal and try to improve it based on the issues and the planning instructions. [OPTIONAL: if truncated_plan_text is provided, include the following] Truncated plan (use this as the current plan context for repair): <TRUNCATED_PLAN_TEXT> === Detected Issues === [CASE A: errors is non-empty] Issues detected by the validator: - <ERROR_1> - <ERROR_2> ... [CASE B: errors is empty] No structural issues were detected by the validator. However, you should still consider the SPIN evaluation feedback above and improve the DAG minimally if needed. === Original Plan === <ORIGINAL_PLAN> Repair rules: - Interpret the SPIN feedback as follows in your planning: * status: how complete and correct the current answer is. * can_answer_now=True: you may safely stop planning and keep the plan minimal. * stop_index: earliest step index after which the plan already supports answering. - Use the SPIN evaluation feedback and the issues above to decide how to fix the plan. (cid:44) - Construct the DAG using the bare minimum number of tasks required to satisfy the user question and constraints. Avoid redundant or unnecessary tasks. (cid:44) - Make the minimal changes necessary; if there is no problem, you MUST output the Original Plan as-is. (cid:44) - Do NOT output any explanation, comments, or markdown.
